Farn Porrucar- PnacrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0586
December 29,2005
Becky Hill
Vice Mayor
Corning City Council
1401 Solano Street
Corning, CA 96021
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-05-222
Dear Ms. Hill:
This letter is in response for your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').1 Please note, the Commission
will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(g)(A), copy
enclosed.) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evalu ate any conduct
thatmay have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only
to prospective actions. Our advice is based on the facts presented in your request; the
Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re Oglesby
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Furthermore, please note that your request for advice and the
Commission's response are public records, which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt ofa proper request for disclosure.
QUESTIONS
1. May you participate in decisions involving the creation of landscape and lighting
districts within the city?
2. May you participate in decisions involving the setting of amounts for commercial and
residential developer fees charged by the city?
' 
t Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
.{@r!


File No. A-05-222
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
I & 2. You may participate in the decisions involving the creation of lighting
districts and the setting of amounts for commercial and.residential developer fees
provided these decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your economic interests.
FACTS
The City of Corning raised its residential developer fees in May 2005. You
deliberated and voted on this decision, after seeking legal advice, andreceiving informal
telephone advice from the FPPC. In May or June of 2O06,the city council will also be
deciding whether to create landscape and lighting districts, as well as imposing
commercial developer fees. In addition, the city council will consider updating the
residential developer fees and will review the amount of the fees on an annual basis.
In a phone call on November 28,2005, you stated that landscape and lighting
districts only apply to single family developments. They are created,on "an individual
development basis." In other words, if a development is of such a size that it deems a
district necessary, the city council decides whether to put one in place. The lighting
district will impose a tax on any new residential housing subdivision. Howevir, you
stated that developer fees are "across-the-board," or imposed throughout the city for
various residential and commercial projects. Furthermore, you state that you currently do
not have any listings for vacant properties that may be affected by such a lighting district.
However, you may have such listings in the future.
Your husband is a real estate broker who runs his own real estate office and
currently employs you as areal estate sales agent and mortgage loan officer. The office
is in an established, colnmercial part of the city that will not be subject to the landscape
and lighting district. Furthermore, you stated that neither you nor your husband pays
these developer fees. In addition, you said that you do not believe that these developer
fees will directly affect you or your husband's income, nor do you believe that you will
see an increase of$50,000 in your business's gross annual receipts.
You are asking for formal written advice because the issue regarding the setting
of developer fees will be a recurring decision that will come before the city council each
year.
ANALYSIS
Conflict-of-Interest Prohibition
Your questions implicate the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions which ensure
that public officials "perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported


File No. A-05-222
Page No. 3
them." (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a goveflrmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests.
(Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step
analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disquali$ring conflict of interest in a
given govemmental decision. (Regulation 1 8700(b)( 1 )-(8).)
I & 2. Are you a 6'public official" and will you be making, participating in
making, or influencrng a governmental decision?
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act pertain only to public officials. A
public official includes "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency . . . ." (Section 82048; regulation 18701(a)(1).) As vice mayor of the
City of Corning, you are a public official subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest
provisions.
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contracfual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See regulation L8702.1.)
You will "make a govemmental decision" if you vote on any issue obligating the
city with respect to the formation of a landscape and lighting district ("District';), orlf
you participate in a decision that sets the amount of residential or commercial developer
fees. In addition to actually voting on these matters, if you engage in negotiations
without significant substantive review, provide advice or make .""o--"rdations, you
will be "participating" in a decision (regulation lS7O2.2). You will also be "influencing,,
that decision if you appear before or otherwise attempt to influence any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the agncy.2 (Regulation 1SIOZ.Z.1
2 If a public official is enumerated in section 87200 (including city council members) and he or
she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (t j immediately
prior to the discussion of the item, orally identifii each type of economic interest involved in ihe decision as
well as details ofthe economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5, subdivision (b)(l)(B), on the
record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the
discussion andlor vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speakiag as a
member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5, subdivisions
(c) and (d) apply. ($ 87105.) Since a city council member is a position enumerated in section 87200, these
requirements apply to you. Please also note the Commission's special rules on attribution of commission
income. (Regulation 18703.3(c), enclosed.)


File No. A-05-222
Page No. 4
3. What are your economic interests - 
the possible sources of a conflict of
interest?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation 1s703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87 I 03 (d); regulation I 8703. I (b));
an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 37103(b); regulation 1g703.2);
o
a
o an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section S7103(c);
regulation 18703.3);
. 
an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregateto $360
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); r"gulution
18703.4);
In addition, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her
personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the ,,personal
financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5.)
Economic rnterests Disclosed in vour Request for Assistance
Real Estate Business:
The business is an economic interest of yours on several bases. you state that
your husband runs areal estate office in the City of Corning and employs you, a real
,estate sales agent and mortgage loan officer. Provided that your spouse's interest in the
business is $2,000 or more, and if the business provides you with irr"o*e - including
community property income -of $500 per year or more, you have an economic interest in
the office as a business and as a source of income. (Section 87103(a) & (c).) In addition,
you are employed by the business. (Section 87103(d).)
Please note that income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any
income of any business entity or trust in which the individual oispouse owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially a 10 percent interest or greater. (This also includes the official,s


File No. A-05-222
Page No. 5
community property interest in his or her spouse's income.) (Section 82030.) You stated
that your husband owns a real estate business. Assuming that your spouse's interest is at
least 10 percent of the business, you will have an economic interest in every client of the
business that pays the firm a sufficient amount such that your pro-rata share of the
income which represents your community property interest equals or exceeds $500.
You have not identified particular customers of your husband's business who
qualifu as sources of income, or who have provided you with income of $500 or more
within the prior l2-month period leading to the govemmental decision at issue. We
therefore do not include them in our analysis, but point out that you must still determine
whether any of these business clients qualify as a source of income and whether a conflict
of interest will arise from any of them (i.e., whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision you participate in will result in a material financial effect on any
of these customers who are economic interests of yours). If you believe that clients may
be affected by the above decisions, you should request additional advice.
Personal Finances:
You also have an economic interest in your personal finances and those of your
immediate family. (Section 87103:' regulation 18703.5.) A governmental decision will
have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will result in the personal
expenses, income, assets or liabilities of an official or his or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing. Qbid.)
Your letter does not mention any impact on your personal finances as the result of
the upcoming decisions, therefore we do not further analyze this issue. HoweveE please
note that a conflict of interest may arise if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions
to create a landscape and lighting district or set developer fees will have an effect on yogr
personal finances.3
4. Are your economic interests directly or indirecfly involved in the
governmental decision?
Business Entity
"A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources
of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official,s agency
when that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; (2) Is a named
3 Ho*ever, when determining whether a govemmental decision has a financial effect on a public
official's economic interest in his or herpersonal finances, a financial effect on the gross revenues,
"ip"rr"s, 
or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment interest shall no t be considered. (Regulation I 8705. 5(a).)


File No. A-05-222
Page No. 6
party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official's ageflcy. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial
or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract
with, the subject person." (Regulation 18704.1, subd. (a).)
Since your spouse's real estate business is not a named party in, or the subject of
the proceedings conceming the proposed landscape and lightirg airtrict, or the setling of
developer fees, the business is indirectly involved in the decision.
5. Applicable Materiality Standard
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on th" nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectlylnvolved in the
agency's decision.
Business Entity:
The financial effect of a governmental decision on a small business entity, which
is not directly involved in the decision, is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The govefllmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 o. rnor.;-or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.1(c)(a).)
This materiality standard is applicable to your husband's real estate business,
which is also a source of income to you.
6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon your economic interests will meet the applicable
materiality standard? (Does a conflict of interest result?)
A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it
is substantially likely that one or more of the mateiality standards applicable to that
economic interest will be met as a result of the govemmental decision. (Regulation


File No. A-05-222
Page No. 7
18706(a).) A financial effect need not be certain to be consideredreasonably
foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FppC
Ops. 198.)
The question of whether financial consequences upon a business entity are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made must always depend
on the facts of each particular case. (In re Thorner, supra.) In each case, the om"iat
must look at the facts surrounding each decision, and the nature of the economic interest
to determine whether id is reasonably foreseeable that the decision is substantially likely
to cause amateial financial effect as set forth above.
In Thorner, the Commission considered a number of factors, including: (1) the
historic pattern of business in the jurisdiction; (2) thebusiness's market share andlor the
existence of substantial competition in the jurisdiction; (3) the existence of affirmative
acts on the part of the official to take advantage of the financial effects of the decision;
(4) the scope of the governmental decision in question; (5) the existence of intervening
effects that must occur before a financial benefit will result.
The application of the conflict-of-interest rules must be carried out on a decision-
by-decision basis and based on the specific facts. We do not have sufficient facts to
determine, using the above factors, whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
to create a landscape and lighting district within the city, or the decision to set rates for
commercial and residential developer fees charged by the city, will have a material
financial effect on your economic interests.
Please note that the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing
advice' (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Therefore, the determination of whether
or not it is reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for
your spouse's real estate business is necessarily afact:ual question that is ultimately for
you to decide.
7. The o6Public Generally, Exception
Even if a public official otherwise has a conflict of interest, he or she may still be
able to take a role in the govemmental decision in question. If the reasonably foieseeable
material financial effect of a govemmental decisior^o, the public official's etonomic
interest is indistinguishable oofrom its effect on the public generally," then the public
official does not have a conflict. (Section 87103; regulations 1S700(bX7y , ttlOl61.7
This rule is referred to as the'public generally,, exception.
There are seven forms of the'public generally" exception: a general exception
(regulation 18707 .L) and six specialized forms of the exception (regulation s 18707 .2 -
18707.9).


File No. A-05-222
Page No. 8
Reeulation 1$707.L: General Rule:
Regulation 18707.1(b) sets forth separate definitions of a "significant segment"
depending on the nafure of the economic interest involved.
Business Enttty:
When a public official's economic interest is a business entity, subdivision
18707.1(b)(1XC) requires that the affected segment of the public comprise more than a
single industry, trade, or profession, in order to fall under the general exception.
Your economic interest is in your husband's real estate office, a business entity.
The proposed landscape and lighting district affects only developers of single family
residences within the City of Corning. Thus, decisions regarding the proposed district
will affect only single family real estate developers, which represent a single industry
within the jurisdiction. Under subdivision 18707.1(bX1XC) a "significant segment,,of
the public generally, as defined by the general exception, will not be affected by these
decisions.
Special RuIe for Rates. Assessments. and Similar Decisionq (18707.2)
Regulation 18707.2 is a specialized form of the "public generally'' exception that
can apply when the decision relates to assessments, taxes, fees, charges, rates, or similar
decisions that are applied proportionally, or "across-the-board" to the official's economic
interest and a significant segment of the jurisdiction as defined in regulation, 18707.1(b).
The material financial effect of a governmental decision on apublic official's
economic interests is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if both
subdivisions (b)(1) and (b)(2) of section 18707.1are met. In other words, the
govefilmental decision must affect a "significant segment" of the public generally and it
must affect that segment in "substantially the same manner.,,
Under subdivision I s707. 1 (b)( 1)(c) a "significant segment" of the public
generally, as defined by the general exception, will not be affected by these decisions
because the assessments affect a single industry within the jurisdiction. Therefore, this
specialized form of the "public generally'' exception is not applicable under your facts.
Regulation 18707.7: Industries. Trades" or professions
Regulation 18707 .7 provides a specialized form of the "public generally''
exception, applicable when a decision will affect a single industry, trade, or profession in
substantially the same manner as the decision will affect an official's economic interest.
Under this specialized form of the exception, a single industry, trade, or profession may
constitute a significant segment of the public only if it is the "predominant industry',
within the official's jurisdiction or in the district represented by the official. The purpose
of the predominant industry exception is ". . . to avoid disqualification in such cases as a


File No. A-05-222
Page No. 9
farmer elected in a rural community in which agriculture is the major industry." (In re
Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops.62.) The exception is limited to the situation where alocal
economy is based on one industry so that almost any public official would have an
economic tie to that industry. (Woods Advice Letter,No. 4-94-164.)
With respect to public officials who are not elected state officers, an industry
trade, or profession that constitutes fifty percent or more of business entities in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents is a
"predominant" industry, trade or profession. (Regulati on 18707.7(b).)
Under the facts that you provide, it would appear unlikely that the real estate
industry constitutes fifty percent or more of all the business entities in the City of
corning and is therefore the predominant industry within the jurisdiction.
8. "Legally Required Participation,, Exception
The "legally required participation" rule applies when the official's participation
in a governmental decision is legally required. (Section 87L}L;regulation iSZOS.) Vo,
have not presented any facts indicating that this exception is applicable to your situation,
therefore we do not address it here.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (el6)
322-5660.
Enclosures
ER:rd

Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
r'
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
1. May you participate in decisions involving the creation of landscape and lighting
districts within the city?
2. May you participate in decisions involving the setting of amounts for commercial and
residential developer fees charged by the city?

sections.conclusion:
I & 2. You may participate in the decisions involving the creation of lighting
districts and the setting of amounts for commercial and.residential developer fees
provided these decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your economic interests.

In a phone call on November 28,2005, you stated that landscape and lighting
districts only apply to single family developments. They are created,on "an individual
development basis." In other words, if a development is of such a size that it deems a
district necessary, the city council decides whether to put one in place. The lighting
district will impose a tax on any new residential housing subdivision. Howevir, you
stated that developer fees are "across-the-board," or imposed throughout the city for
various residential and commercial projects. Furthermore, you state that you currently do
not have any listings for vacant properties that may be affected by such a lighting district.
However, you may have such listings in the future.
Your husband is a real estate broker who runs his own real estate office and
currently employs you as areal estate sales agent and mortgage loan officer. The office
is in an established, colnmercial part of the city that will not be subject to the landscape
and lighting district. Furthermore, you stated that neither you nor your husband pays
these developer fees. In addition, you said that you do not believe that these developer
fees will directly affect you or your husband's income, nor do you believe that you will

year.

meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will

analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disquali$ring conflict of interest in a
given govemmental decision. (Regulation 1 8700(b)( 1 )-(8).)

making, or influencrng a governmental decision?

government agency . . . ." (Section 82048; regulation 18701(a)(1).) As vice mayor of the

provisions.
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contracfual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See regulation L8702.1.)
You will "make a govemmental decision" if you vote on any issue obligating the
city with respect to the formation of a landscape and lighting district ("District';), orlf
you participate in a decision that sets the amount of residential or commercial developer
fees. In addition to actually voting on these matters, if you engage in negotiations
without significant substantive review, provide advice or make .""o--"rdations, you
will be "participating" in a decision (regulation lS7O2.2). You will also be "influencing,,
that decision if you appear before or otherwise attempt to influence any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the agncy.2 (Regulation 1SIOZ.Z.1

she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (t j immediately
prior to the discussion of the item, orally identifii each type of economic interest involved in ihe decision as
well as details ofthe economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5, subdivision (b)(l)(B), on the
record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the
discussion andlor vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speakiag as a

the possible sources of a conflict of
interest?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation 1s703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87 I 03 (d); regulation I 8703. I (b));
an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 37103(b); regulation 1g703.2);
o
a
o an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section S7103(c);
regulation 18703.3);
. 
an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregateto $360
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); r"gulution
18703.4);
In addition, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her

financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5.)
Economic rnterests Disclosed in vour Request for Assistance
Real Estate Business:
The business is an economic interest of yours on several bases. you state that
your husband runs areal estate office in the City of Corning and employs you, a real
,estate sales agent and mortgage loan officer. Provided that your spouse's interest in the
business is $2,000 or more, and if the business provides you with irr"o*e - including
community property income -of $500 per year or more, you have an economic interest in
the office as a business and as a source of income. (Section 87103(a) & (c).) In addition,
you are employed by the business. (Section 87103(d).)
Please note that income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any
income of any business entity or trust in which the individual oispouse owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially a 10 percent interest or greater. (This also includes the official,s
community property interest in his or her spouse's income.) (Section 82030.) You stated
that your husband owns a real estate business. Assuming that your spouse's interest is at
least 10 percent of the business, you will have an economic interest in every client of the
business that pays the firm a sufficient amount such that your pro-rata share of the
income which represents your community property interest equals or exceeds $500.
You have not identified particular customers of your husband's business who
qualifu as sources of income, or who have provided you with income of $500 or more
within the prior l2-month period leading to the govemmental decision at issue. We
therefore do not include them in our analysis, but point out that you must still determine
whether any of these business clients qualify as a source of income and whether a conflict
of interest will arise from any of them (i.e., whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision you participate in will result in a material financial effect on any
of these customers who are economic interests of yours). If you believe that clients may
be affected by the above decisions, you should request additional advice.
Personal Finances:
You also have an economic interest in your personal finances and those of your
immediate family. (Section 87103:' regulation 18703.5.) A governmental decision will
have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will result in the personal
expenses, income, assets or liabilities of an official or his or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing. Qbid.)
Your letter does not mention any impact on your personal finances as the result of
the upcoming decisions, therefore we do not further analyze this issue. HoweveE please
note that a conflict of interest may arise if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions
to create a landscape and lighting district or set developer fees will have an effect on yogr
personal finances.3

governmental decision?

of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official,s agency
when that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; (2) Is a named

"ip"rr"s, 
or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment interest shall no t be considered. (Regulation I 8705. 5(a).)
party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official's ageflcy. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial
or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract
with, the subject person." (Regulation 18704.1, subd. (a).)
Since your spouse's real estate business is not a named party in, or the subject of
the proceedings conceming the proposed landscape and lightirg airtrict, or the setling of
developer fees, the business is indirectly involved in the decision.
5. Applicable Materiality Standard
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on th" nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectlylnvolved in the
agency's decision.
Business Entity:
The financial effect of a governmental decision on a small business entity, which
is not directly involved in the decision, is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The govefllmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 o. rnor.;-or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.1(c)(a).)
This materiality standard is applicable to your husband's real estate business,
which is also a source of income to you.
6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon your economic interests will meet the applicable
materiality standard? (Does a conflict of interest result?)
A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it
is substantially likely that one or more of the mateiality standards applicable to that
economic interest will be met as a result of the govemmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) A financial effect need not be certain to be consideredreasonably
foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FppC
Ops. 198.)
The question of whether financial consequences upon a business entity are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made must always depend
on the facts of each particular case. (In re Thorner, supra.) In each case, the om"iat
must look at the facts surrounding each decision, and the nature of the economic interest
to determine whether id is reasonably foreseeable that the decision is substantially likely
to cause amateial financial effect as set forth above.
In Thorner, the Commission considered a number of factors, including: (1) the
historic pattern of business in the jurisdiction; (2) thebusiness's market share andlor the
existence of substantial competition in the jurisdiction; (3) the existence of affirmative
acts on the part of the official to take advantage of the financial effects of the decision;
(4) the scope of the governmental decision in question; (5) the existence of intervening
effects that must occur before a financial benefit will result.
The application of the conflict-of-interest rules must be carried out on a decision-
by-decision basis and based on the specific facts. We do not have sufficient facts to
determine, using the above factors, whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
to create a landscape and lighting district within the city, or the decision to set rates for
commercial and residential developer fees charged by the city, will have a material
financial effect on your economic interests.
Please note that the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing
advice' (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Therefore, the determination of whether
or not it is reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for
your spouse's real estate business is necessarily afact:ual question that is ultimately for
you to decide.
7. The o6Public Generally, Exception
Even if a public official otherwise has a conflict of interest, he or she may still be
able to take a role in the govemmental decision in question. If the reasonably foieseeable
material financial effect of a govemmental decisior^o, the public official's etonomic
interest is indistinguishable oofrom its effect on the public generally," then the public
official does not have a conflict. (Section 87103; regulations 1S700(bX7y , ttlOl61.7
This rule is referred to as the'public generally,, exception.
There are seven forms of the'public generally" exception: a general exception
(regulation 18707 .L) and six specialized forms of the exception (regulation s 18707 .2 -
18707.9).
Reeulation 1$707.L: General Rule:
Regulation 18707.1(b) sets forth separate definitions of a "significant segment"
depending on the nafure of the economic interest involved.
Business Enttty:
When a public official's economic interest is a business entity, subdivision
18707.1(b)(1XC) requires that the affected segment of the public comprise more than a
single industry, trade, or profession, in order to fall under the general exception.
Your economic interest is in your husband's real estate office, a business entity.
The proposed landscape and lighting district affects only developers of single family
residences within the City of Corning. Thus, decisions regarding the proposed district
will affect only single family real estate developers, which represent a single industry
within the jurisdiction. Under subdivision 18707.1(bX1XC) a "significant segment,,of
the public generally, as defined by the general exception, will not be affected by these
decisions.
Special RuIe for Rates. Assessments. and Similar Decisionq (18707.2)
Regulation 18707.2 is a specialized form of the "public generally'' exception that
can apply when the decision relates to assessments, taxes, fees, charges, rates, or similar
decisions that are applied proportionally, or "across-the-board" to the official's economic
interest and a significant segment of the jurisdiction as defined in regulation, 18707.1(b).
The material financial effect of a governmental decision on apublic official's
economic interests is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if both
subdivisions (b)(1) and (b)(2) of section 18707.1are met. In other words, the
govefilmental decision must affect a "significant segment" of the public generally and it
must affect that segment in "substantially the same manner.,,
Under subdivision I s707. 1 (b)( 1)(c) a "significant segment" of the public
generally, as defined by the general exception, will not be affected by these decisions
because the assessments affect a single industry within the jurisdiction. Therefore, this
specialized form of the "public generally'' exception is not applicable under your facts.
Regulation 18707.7: Industries. Trades" or professions
Regulation 18707 .7 provides a specialized form of the "public generally''
exception, applicable when a decision will affect a single industry, trade, or profession in
substantially the same manner as the decision will affect an official's economic interest.
Under this specialized form of the exception, a single industry, trade, or profession may
constitute a significant segment of the public only if it is the "predominant industry',
within the official's jurisdiction or in the district represented by the official. The purpose
of the predominant industry exception is ". . . to avoid disqualification in such cases as a
farmer elected in a rural community in which agriculture is the major industry." (In re
Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops.62.) The exception is limited to the situation where alocal
economy is based on one industry so that almost any public official would have an
economic tie to that industry. (Woods Advice Letter,No. 4-94-164.)
With respect to public officials who are not elected state officers, an industry
trade, or profession that constitutes fifty percent or more of business entities in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents is a
"predominant" industry, trade or profession. (Regulati on 18707.7(b).)
Under the facts that you provide, it would appear unlikely that the real estate
industry constitutes fifty percent or more of all the business entities in the City of
corning and is therefore the predominant industry within the jurisdiction.
8. "Legally Required Participation,, Exception
The "legally required participation" rule applies when the official's participation
in a governmental decision is legally required. (Section 87L}L;regulation iSZOS.) Vo,
have not presented any facts indicating that this exception is applicable to your situation,
therefore we do not address it here.

embedding.first_500_words: Farn Porrucar- PnacrrcEs CouvrrssroN 428 J Street . Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0586 December 29,2005 Becky Hill Vice Mayor Corning City Council 1401 Solano Street Corning, CA 96021 Re: Your Request for Advice Our FiIe No. A-05-222 Dear Ms. Hill: This letter is in response for your request for advice regarding the conflict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').1 Please note, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(g)(A), copy enclosed.) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evalu ate any conduct thatmay have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. Our advice is based on the facts presented in your request; the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Furthermore, please note that your request for advice and the Commission's response are public records, which may be disclosed to the public upon receipt ofa proper request for disclosure. QUESTIONS 1. May you participate in decisions involving the creation of landscape and lighting districts within the city? 2. May you participate in decisions involving the setting of amounts for commercial and residential developer fees charged by the city? ' t Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. .{@r! File No. A-05-222 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS I & 2. You may participate in the decisions involving the creation of lighting districts and the setting of amounts for commercial and.residential developer fees provided these decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests. FACTS The City of Corning raised its residential developer fees in May 2005. You deliberated and voted on this decision, after seeking legal advice, andreceiving informal telephone advice from the FPPC. In May or June of 2O06,the city council will also be deciding whether to create landscape and lighting districts, as well as imposing commercial developer fees. In addition, the city council will consider updating the residential developer fees and will review the amount of the fees on an annual basis. In a phone call on November 28,2005, you stated that landscape and lighting districts only apply to single family developments. They are created,on "an individual development basis." In other words, if a development is of such a size that it deems a district necessary, the city council decides whether to put one in place. The lighting district will impose a tax on any new residential housing subdivision. Howevir, you stated that developer fees are "across-the-board," or imposed throughout the city for various residential and commercial projects. Furthermore, you state that you currently do not have any listings for vacant properties that may be affected by such a lighting district. However, you may have such listings in the future. Your husband is a real estate broker who runs his own real estate office
